data.title: Diamond Hill Short Duration Investment Grade Strategy Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

The Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index returned +1.21% in Q2, adding to the strong performance in Q1 (+2.78%) and bringing year-to-date performance for the index to +4.02%. The 2025 year-to-date performance represents the best six-month start to a calendar year since 2020’s return of +6.14%.

The story of the second quarter was all about the journey and not the destination, as the markets were roiled by Liberation Day and higher-than-expected tariffs across the board. The S&P 500 posted its fifth-largest two-day decline since World War II, but ended the quarter at a record high as tariff delays and alterations mitigated most of the damage wrought early in the quarter. The fixed income markets followed a similar path, dropping precipitously in the early days of April before rallying in the latter part of the quarter.

Q2 was one of the most chaotic and unsettling quarters in recent memory as the market dealt with the fallout from Liberation Day, oil price volatility driven by the short-lived Israel-Iran conflict and US fiscal concerns. The combination of the passage of the Big Beautiful Bill, as well as the final nail in the AAA US credit quality coffin, with Moody’s finally joining S&P and Fitch in downgrading US debt to AA+, led to a move higher on the longer end of the Treasury curve. Adding to the chaos of the quarter was the ongoing name-calling and demand for the resignation of Jerome Powell, making Q2 one for the ages. But despite the tumultuous journey, the result was relatively strong performance across the board.

Exhibit 1 — Cumulative Performance Q2 2025 (%)

Source: Bloomberg. IG Corp – Investment grade corporates. Securitized – Bloomberg US Securitized Index. Treasury – 10Y US Treasury Yield. The MOVE Index tracks volatility in the US Treasury market in order to provide insight into the fixed income markets. It utilizes options-pricing models based on a weighted average of option probabilities to reflect expectations for future volatility in fixed income markets.

It was a challenging start to the quarter as Liberation Day delivered significantly higher and broader tariffs than the market had been expecting. A flat across-the-board 10% tariff for all countries, with higher rates for specific countries based on the overall trade relationship. As we all know, markets dislike uncertainty, and this was reflected in the performance across all risk assets with investors pricing in a global downturn. The Treasury market was no different, with the yield on the 10-year Treasury and 30-year Treasury jumping 50 basis points (bps) and 46 bps, respectively, during the week following Liberation Day. This disruption led to the 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs, triggering a massive rally. As the quarter progressed, markets fell prey to every news flash and announcement, ranging from the back-and-forth tariff escalation between China and the US to rulings from the US Court of International Trade.

Amid all the volatility tied to the global trade situation, Moody’s finally joined S&P (August 2011) and Fitch (August 2023) in downgrading US government debt from AAA to AA+/Aa1. Moody’s cited the government’s inability to agree on measures to reverse the trend of large fiscal deficits and ever-increasing interest costs. The announcement from Moody’s felt like a non-event in the markets, almost as if the broad response was, “What took so long for you to catch up with the rest of the world?”. Compounding the concerns about the fiscal situation was the passage of Trump’s signature bill that extends tax cuts, launches a new child savings account and increases spending on immigration reform and military spending. However, the reductions don’t meet or exceed the increase in spending, resulting in additional debt being added to the already substantial US budget deficit. According to the Congressional Budget Office, the impact of the bill’s passage would be an increase in the deficit of $1.7 trillion over the next five years and an increase of $2.4 trillion over the next ten years.

In the face of the turmoil besetting the market throughout the quarter, economic data remained resilient. Various economic data points continued to indicate that the economy was expanding and on somewhat solid ground. The labor market generated an average of 154,000 jobs per month, including revisions, while the unemployment rate remained at 4.2% in April and May before slightly decreasing to 4.1%. While concerns persist regarding the impact of tariffs on inflation, core inflation remained at 2.8% throughout the quarter, following a 3.1% finish in the first quarter. The Federal Reserve’s two main responsibilities, price stability and employment, remained in manageable territory and kept the Fed on the sidelines during its two meetings during the quarter.

While the Federal Reserve has remained on the sidelines, they have not stayed out of the administration’s crosshairs. Continued calls from President Trump for the resignation of Jerome Powell have fueled the financial news networks, but do not seem to have rattled the Chair. The prospect of the Shadow Fed Chair has been discussed, which essentially would be the nomination or designation of Powell’s successor before his term expires (May 2026). However, even the prospect of a Shadow Fed Chair does not seem sufficient to derail the Fed’s current approach of patience and data dependency. Monetary policy is determined by the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC), not the Fed Chair themself. Powell is one of twelve voting members on the FOMC, and possible replacements would likely maintain the institution’s autonomy. While any Shadow Fed Chair could create some uncertainty risk in the market, the power still resides with the FOMC.

Exhibit 2 — Powell Has Been a Consensus Builder (%)

Source: Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis. Data represents the percentage of FOMC voting members who voted in favor of monetary policy directives set by the Presiding Chair of the FOMC.

Treasury Update

While the Treasury market delivered a positive performance during the quarter (+0.85% for the Bloomberg US Treasury Index), it lagged both the corporate and securitized sectors, pulling the overall performance of the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index lower. The main culprit for the drop in performance was the twisting of the curve as the shorter end yield moved lower while the longer end pushed higher. The 2-year Treasury yield dropped from 3.89% to 3.72% during the quarter, while the 30- year Treasury yield climbed from 4.59% to 4.78% as concerns about the fiscal situation intensified. The impact of the yield shift is evident in the performance of the Treasury Bellwether securities: the 2-year Treasury Bellwether returned +1.12% and the 30-year Treasury Bellwether returned -2.08% in Q2.

The December Fed funds futures moved nearly in lockstep with the 2-year Treasury, shifting throughout the month as incoming economic data and trade gyrations fueled the moves through the quarter. The volatility during the quarter resulted in significant action across the curve, despite the fact that quarter-over-quarter movements in rates were somewhat muted. Consider that the 2-year Treasury finished 17 bps lower during the quarter but ranged from as low as 3.60% to a peak of 4.05%, a range of 45 bps. The longer end of the curve experienced even greater ranges, with the 10-year yield moving higher by only one basis point but covering a range of roughly 61 bps during the quarter. The 30-year Treasury yield rose 19 bps from the end of March to the end of June but fluctuated between 4.41% and 5.09% before settling at 4.78% at quarter-end.

Exhibit 3 — Cumulative Implied Rate Change Through Year-End (%)

Source: Bloomberg.

Corporate Update

Much like the rest of the market, the investment-grade corporate sector experienced significant fluctuations throughout the quarter but ended June with spread levels tighter than where they began. Spread is the additional yield over comparable duration Treasuries that compensates investors for the additional credit risk. Investment-grade corporate spreads, as measured by the Bloomberg US Corporate Bond Index, traded in a range from 118.5 bps to 83.2 bps, which is where the index finished the quarter. The spread level for the index reached 118.5 bps in the days following Liberation Day, as perceived risk increased with the uncertainty of the impact of tariffs on the corporate market.

The yield on the corporate index followed a similar path, jumping roughly +0.40% from the beginning of below 5.00% by the end of June. Despite the volatility during the quarter, investors continue to clamor for the sector, as the overall yield it provides remains attractive. However, spread levels in the corporate market remain tight, both historically and relative to other areas of the market.

Exhibit 4 — Investment Grade Corporate Spreads (bps)

Source: Bloomberg. Bloomberg US Corporate Bond Index vs Treasury Yield.

Securitized Update

Fluctuations in the rates market led to a divergence within the mortgage market, as better-structured collateralized mortgage obligations (CMOs) held up much better than plain-vanilla mortgage passthrough securities. CMOs, as measured by the ICE BofA CMO Index, returned +1.48% while passthroughs, as measured by the Bloomberg US RMBS Index, returned +1.14%. Elsewhere within the securitized market, non-Agency commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) continued to deliver decent performance (+2.00% during the quarter) while shorter-duration asset-backed securities (ABS) (+1.38%) continued the slow and steady pace of shorter-duration securities with attractive spread levels.

Despite the volatility in the corporate sector during the quarter, spreads within that sector remain tight from a historical standpoint, and better-yielding and structured securitized segments of the market continue to offer better relative value for managers willing to dig into the details of the structures and underlying collateral. Issuance in the securitized market remains strong, despite the slowdown experienced in mid-April. While off the pace from the record levels of 2024, 2025 is issuing at a run rate that would place production near the record level and comfortably in second position historically.

Consumer-related structured product remittance reports indicate a stabilizing consumer, with 30-day delinquencies for subprime and prime auto loans increasing by 0.1% to 14.6% and 1.8%, respectively, while credit card and retail card delinquencies decrease by 0.2% (to 1.3%) and 0.3% (to 3.2%), respectively. Delinquencies in consumer personal loans decreased in Traditional loans (-0.4% to 5.8%) and remained flat in Marketplace Lending at 4.5%. Over the last twelve months, Subprime Auto has increased by 0.6% and Retail Card has increased by 0.4% while Marketplace Lending has decreased by 0.7% with Prime Auto, Traditional Consumer Loans and Credit Card fairly stable.

Portfolio Positioning and Performance

It is important to note our portfolio works to provide yield for investors while focusing on the shorter end of fixed income markets. We believe there are opportunities to add incremental yield over the benchmark by investing in structured products across the investment-grade quality spectrum.

As of the end of the second quarter, the portfolio had a yield-to-worst (YTW) of 5.57% with an effective duration of 1.40 years, compared to a YTW of 5.58% and an effective duration of 1.39 years at the end of the first quarter. The decrease in yield is reflective of the shift lower in the yield curve as well as overall spread tightening since the end of the first quarter, despite the volatility early in the second quarter.

The portfolio’s allocation to the corporate sector detracted from performance as the allocation trailed the comparable benchmark allocation and was also an underweight. The Treasury sector detracted from relative performance with the portfolio allocation delivering strong relative returns during the quarter, but this was offset by the underweight as the benchmark is roughly 70.5% in Treasury securities.

Spreads on the shorter end of the corporate curve finished the quarter at compressed levels and remain tight relative to various areas of the securitized sector. Performance in the securitized allocation was driven by continued strength in the non- agency residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS) and CMBS sectors, both of which continue to benefit from investors’ search for yield in markets that continue to recover from prior years’ challenges.

Within non-agency CMBS, areas such as Single Asset Single Borrower (SASB) securities and commercial real estate collateralized loan obligations (CRE CLOs) led the way, from office to industrial, while the ABS allocation was fueled by strong performance in the auto and unsecured consumer allocations. With interest rates on a roller-coaster ride during the quarter, well-structured CMOs held up well and contributed to overall performance.

The duration of the portfolio remains positioned near the midpoint of target duration of 1 to 2.

We continue to search for opportunities in the marketplace while maintaining an attractive yield relative to the benchmark.

Bonds rated AAA, AA, A and BBB are considered investment grade.

Team

data.title: Silver Is A Sleeping Giant
data.description:
I keep underlining that gold is the currency of global conflict, and as global governments wake up to the never-ending cycle of increasing tension, they are laying in gold in case of disaster. Gold has become the metal of governments; silver remains the metal of retail. As geopolitical tensions amplify state gold accumulation, silver is poised to catch up as retail cottons on. History shows that when silver moves, it moves fast. With retail participation returning, silver’s asymmetric potential could set up a run toward $100 per ounce.

Gold vs. Silver: Diverging Drivers

For centuries, both gold and silver were viewed as stores of wealth: gold for caches, silver for large transactions, and copper for pocket change. Today, that dynamic has shifted:

Gold demand is now government-driven: central banks are hoarding gold as a hedge against geopolitical instability risk.

Silver demand is retail-driven, and retail is deciding to jump on precious metals, with silver a retail-friendly alternative to ‘pricey’ gold.

This divergence creates an unusual setup: gold rallies on sovereign stockpiling, while silver lags… until retail piles in.

The Catch-Up Pattern

In the past decade, gold’s outperformance of silver hasn’t been linear. The 2020 repricing demonstrated silver’s tendency to surge in catch-up bursts.

A similar setup may now be forming:

Gold’s steady appreciation underwrites confidence in the metals trade. Silver’s underperformance creates a relative value gap that can be filled. When the gap becomes wide, silver revalues sharply.

If gold continues its climb toward the $4,000–$5,000 range, silver can follow in proportion, implying $50–$60 and potentially higher.

The Structural Imbalance

Global production highlights the anomaly. Annual mining output is approximately:

Gold: ~3,200 tonnes

Silver: ~25,000 tonnes

That’s an 8:1 production ratio, closer to 10:1 if simplified. Historically, gold-to-silver price ratios were much wider, you have to go back 2000 years for that sort of intrinsic pricing ratio. Today’s ratios, hovering around 80-90 to 1, suggest structural mispricing. If retail investors follow governments into precious metals, silver could see that yawning gap close, which would create a dramatic move.

Geopolitics As Catalyst

Markets don’t move on supply-demand logic alone; they respond to catalysts. Current signals include:

U.S.–China–Russia tensions are escalating, reinforcing the gold bid.

Political rhetoric amplifying the sense of risk (e.g., Trump’s recent remarks about global adversaries are almost unbelievable, even in this febrile period).

Precious metals as “war assets” — with gold leading and silver following.

In a de-escalation scenario, precious metals will pull back, but where is that good news coming from? This trend of increasing tension seems to have only just gotten underway.

Risks

Silver volatility: Retail-driven demand means silver is prone to sharp swings. For investors, that’s trying, but for traders, that’s music to their ears.

Global slowdown: Silver’s industrial demand could soften in recessionary conditions, but it’s counterbalanced by the new demands for silver that AI will stoke.

Positioning extremes: Overcrowded retail trades can unwind quickly, but that setup is likely to only be a threat at much higher levels.

The Case for $100 Silver

Silver once was the “fast horse” in the precious metals race, and if retail gets a lust for precious metals to mirror the ascent of gold, then it will go from sleepy dray to flying Pegasus. With gold being vacuumed into government hands and silver still dominated by retail, the setup favors another catch-up cycle. A move toward $100 per ounce may seem ambitious, but history shows silver can sprint.

Investors should recognize the asymmetric reward-to-risk profile. In a world marching toward heightened geopolitical conflict, silver is highly likely to have a major ascent.

As such, I have been aggressively acquiring physical, ETFs, and silver stocks.

The day sanity breaks out, I will be a seller, and may that day come soon. Sadly, I do not see that in the foreseeable future.

data.title: Gold Market Commentary: Stubborn Stagflation
data.description:
Gold closing in on new highs

A strong rally into month-end saw gold reach US$3,429/oz (+4%), and as of the end of August, gold was up 31% for the year. Gold gained in all major currencies, despite a much weaker US dollar (Table 1). And the positive momentum has carried on in early September.

Our Gold Return Attribution Model (GRAM) suggests major contributors to August price performance were a drop in the US dollar early in the month, continued geopolitical tensions, and strong global gold ETF flows (Chart 1). More recently, a higher chance of a September rate cut has also played a role.

Gold ETF flows provided plenty of support, especially late in the month, posting US$5.5bn (53t) of inflows, dominated by North America (US$4.1bn) and Europe (US$1.9bn), while Asia and other regions saw outflows. COMEX managed money net longs saw more restrained inflows of US$2bn (+16t).

Chart 1: A US dollar reversal, broad gold ETF gains and Trump-Fed tensions helped drive gold close to ATH in August

Key drivers of gold’s return by month

Table 1: Gold posted solid gains across all major currencies in August

Gold price and performance in key currencies*

USD(oz) EUR(oz) JPY(g) GBP(oz) CAD(oz) CHF(oz) INR(10g) RMB(g) TRY(oz) AUD(oz)
August price* 3,429 2,934 16,212 2,539 4,712 2,745 101,967 782 141,091 5,234
August return* 3.9% 1.6% 1.4% 1.8% 3.1% 2.4% 4.0% 2.0% 5.4% 2.2%
Y-t-d return* 31.4% 16.4% 22.8% 21.8% 25.5% 15.9% 34.3% 26.9% 52.9% 24.3%
Record high price* 3,435 3,006 16,079 2,575 4,743 2,812 100,130 830 138,096 5,393
Record high date* 13-Jun-25 22-Apr-25 23-Jul-25 22-Apr-25 22-Apr-25 22-Apr-25 23-Jul-25 22-Apr-25 23-Jul-25 22-Apr-25

*As of 31 August 2025. Based on the LBMA Gold Price PM in USD, expressed in local currencies, except for India and China where the MCX Gold Price PM and Shanghai Gold Benchmark PM are used, respectively.

Source: Bloomberg, World Gold Council

Stubborn stagflation

  • US real rates may become more influential for gold in the near term as US investors grab the baton from emerging markets, and that influence could increase if rates were to fall
  • So far rates have been sticky, but that is more reflective of a growing unease about stagflation
  • Our quantitative analysis of various US investor types suggests that stagflation is of greatest concern to ETF investors, followed by retail bar and coin buyers. Fast money futures investors are more concerned with rate trajectory.

Chart 2: US investors grabbing the baton

Quarterly spot gold returns per intraday trading session

Passing the baton

The relationship between the price of gold and its core drivers shifts over time, sometimes reflecting who is most active in the market.

For example, US real interest rates (opportunity cost) were tightly linked to movements in gold between 2007 and 2022. Last month we suggested that one reason for gold’s decoupling from rates post-2022 was the preponderance of emerging market demand from central banks and other investors, rather than a breakdown in US investor relationship with rates.

Now that central banks and Asian investors have stepped back a bit, as indicated by our Gold Demand Trends data, local premia and intraday session returns (Chart 2), a tighter gold-rates relationship could re-establish itself and Western investors (particularly the US) could become more dominant in driving short-term returns.

Should rates across the curve start to drop, a ramp-up in gold buying could be triggered in the US. But we’re not seeing that quite yet. In fact, the curve is steepening as the short end drops on Fed cut hopes, but the long end remains high on risk premia and future inflation concerns (Chart 3).

Chart 3: Between a rock and a hard place

Fed rate cut expectations and US inflation expectations

The sticky long end might hinder some rates-driven interest in gold. However, a rise in inflation that is concurrent with a slowdown in economic activity1 and weakening labour markets signals we are increasingly flirting with a stagflationary environment. And this tends to favour gold. But which investors are most sensitive to such an environment?

What’s your flavour?

Our analysis suggests that ETF investors are the most sensitive to expectations of stagflation – statistically, significantly so (Chart 4). Bar and coin investors are next, although the average response is not statistically significant. On COMEX, non-reportable investors – who are said to be more representative of retail flows – have also responded positively, on average. But ‘fast money’ investors, many of whom are Commodity Trading Advisors (CTAs) appear less enamoured by stagflationary fears.

This is possibly because they are more focused on interest rates – as we surmised last month. And, for CTAs, technical factors arguably play a role too. In other words, stagflation threatens higher rates, not lower as we are seeing at the moment, and fast money investors are perhaps less willing to participate until those start to soften.

Chart 4: What’s your flavour? Investors’ estimated sensitivity to stagflationary pressure

Demand response during quarters when expected stagflation is high

In summary

Gold’s sensitivity to US real interest rates may increase as Western investors, particularly in the US, take a more active role amid softer demand from emerging markets.

While rates in the long end remain sticky, this reflects growing stagflation concerns – an environment that has historically supported gold. Among US investor segments, ETF holders show the strongest response to stagflation risks and have picked up their pace of investment over the last few weeks, not just in the US but in Europe too – where real rates are still rising – suggesting risk drivers are offsetting rates-based drivers.

Futures traders appear more focused on rate dynamics. As the yield curve steepens (driven by lower front-end rates) and inflation fears persist, the interplay between macroeconomic signals and investor behaviour will be key in shaping gold’s next move.

Footnotes

1US goods trade deficit widens sharply in July | Reuters

data.title: VICI Properties: What Vegas Tourism Declines Mean For Investors
data.description:
Las Vegas, one of the hottest tourist destinations in the world, has seen its visitor count drop for the sixth straight month, down 12% Y/Y in July.

That stat stands in some contradiction to the resiliency and stability expectation that VICI Properties (VICI) investors are looking for.

I wrote multiple articles detailing my view that VICI is not a market-beating investment, yet it is a very reasonable one to make for certain investors given its predictability and relative safety as the dominant property owner on the Las Vegas strip.

And yet, despite the weakness in recent months, I argue VICI remains one of the safest dividend payers in the market. I also think it's important to understand what role VICI plays in the industry, and why it's not as sensitive to tourism weakness as one might think.

Let's dive in.

Introduction To VICI

VICI Properties is a real estate investment trust, or REIT, that focuses on experiential properties. It owns the largest portfolio of properties across the Las Vegas strip, including Caesars Palace, MGM Grand, and the Venetian.

All up, VICI owns 97 assets across North America, of which 54 are gaming properties (i.e. casinos), and 43 diversified experiential assets like bowling alleys, golf courses, and wellness resorts, including Cabot, Canyon Ranch, and Chelsea Piers.

VICI went public as part of the original Caesars Palace bankruptcy, which split the property ownership from the operator of the casino and hotel.

Since its IPO in 2018, VICI has been one of the few REITs that has been able to keep up with the market's total return, though it's notable that the majority of gains have been made up to 2022.

I argue that the strong returns had a lot to do with undervaluation at the IPO, and huge irreplicable moves made by management, including the acquisition of MGM Growth Properties.

In two years of covering VICI on Seeking Alpha, I maintained a 'Buy' rating on the stock, with the important disclaimer that I don't expect it to provide market-beating returns, given its lack of significant growth prospects.

However, I stated that for a certain type of investor, one that is looking for a decent starting yield, dividend growth, and low downside, VICI is a strong addition.

In light of the recent Vegas slump, the question that arises is whether this puts a damper on the thesis.

Las Vegas Slump, And How It Affects VICI

Visitors to Vegas declined 12% in July, marking the sixth consecutive month of year-over-year declines. Total visitors through July were 22.6 million, down 8% Y/Y. Meanwhile, Labor Day weekend, which is typically one of the busiest holidays in Vegas, is estimated to see a 2% drop in visitors.

Of course, the drop in visitors is translating into lower occupancy rates in hotels, with an estimated 17% decline in July, down to 67%.

There could be several explanations for the decline, including the rise of online gambling across the US, economic slowdown, lofty prices, popularity downtrend, or lower inbound travel to the US amid a semi-trade war.

I think it's reasonable to believe each of those has contributed something to the declines.

Meanwhile, gaming revenue, a key source of income for hotel and casino operators, has been more resilient, though it has been flat Y/Y in the twelve months that ended in July.

How This Affects VICI?

First off, an important reminder, VICI's source of revenue is rent paid by its tenants, which is fixed and doesn't include a revenue-sharing arrangement, at least not at a meaningful scale.

As such, VICI is far less sensitive to the recent slump. If, at all, VICI could see second-order effects.

If its tenants struggle, the risk of them being unable to pay is rising. However, I argue this is really a theoretical argument, as VICI's tenants are very far from being unable to pay. In fact, we saw that even during COVID, VICI collected 100% of its rent, and the recent slump is not remotely close to resembling COVID levels.

The only real effect I see, is that some of VICI's options and land lots could be worth a bit less. Some examples are the right to call the Caesars Forum Convention Center, or the right of first refusal to certain strip assets, including land.

That said, I think that 99% of investors are evaluating VICI based on FFO multiples, rather than net asset value, and I don't view the recent down cycle as material enough to justify a lower FFO multiple.

Results Remain Steady As A Rock

VICI's asset portfolio is extremely unique and differentiated, which I argue makes it one of few REITs with an actual moat. The strength of the portfolio, and its size, make VICI's results very boring.

At the end of the day, it's extremely unlikely we'll see VICI's occupancy rates or rent collection fall below 100%. And, with the absence of many remaining acquisition opportunities that are large enough to move the needle, VICI's results should move alongside its rent escalators, i.e., 2%-3% annually, with occasional higher levels due to expansion projects or small acquisitions.

The June quarter was no exception, with AFFO per share rising 5.2% Y/Y, roughly in line with adjusted EBITDA growth, with a small offset from dilution. Occupancy rates were 100%, and rent collection was also 100%.

Looking ahead, VICI upgraded its AFFO guidance from $2,470-$2,500 million to $2,500-$2,520 million, while dilution is expected to be slightly higher than prior guidance, leading to a new AFFO per share guide of $2.35-$2.37.

Valuation

Similar to regular equities, where the stock price generally follows earnings over the long term, VICI's stock price generally follows its FFO per share growth.

I think it's reasonable to expect FFO per share will continue to grow about 3%-5% annually, similar to Q2. In addition, investors get a 5.1% starting yield (a bit higher as VICI just raised its dividend by 4%). That adds up to about 8%-10% expected annual returns.

Lastly, a possible cherry on top, can be multiple expansions. At $33.3 per share, VICI trades at 14 times forward P/AFFO. That's slightly below the sector median, and roughly in line with VICI's historical levels.

As such, I don't expect significant expansion, though if treasury yields, inflation, and Fed rate cuts align, multiple expansion is possible.

Conclusion

The ongoing Las Vegas tourist slump, though certainly not good for VICI, shouldn't be a big concern for investors in my opinion.

VICI's tenants are very strong, and gaming revenue remains stable. I think that, worst-case scenario, VICI could experience some second-order effects if the slump continues for years, which I find unlikely.

At current valuation, VICI offers 8%-10% expected annual returns, and unparalleled predictability. As such, I reiterate the stock as a 'Buy' for dividend growth investors.

data.title: PowerFleet: Inflection Point In The Company, Not In The Share Price
data.description:
PowerFleet (AIOT) merged with MiX last year, which provided its device-agnostic asset-tracking Unity platform with the necessary scale to accommodate much larger customers. At the same time, the company is shifting from hardware to software (SaaS) sales, while reaping acquisition synergies.

We see the multiple advantages emerging:

  • The scaled-up platform is now playing in the A-League, attracting big customers and partners.
  • The shift towards SaaS is ongoing, now reaching 83% of revenue.
  • The company is introducing new products.
  • There is significant upside to ARPU (Average Revenue Per Unit)
  • The cost synergies are emerging.

New customers and partners

As the slide above indicates, there is increasing adoption of Unity's safety and compliance modules across Fortune 500 companies in verticals like food and beverage and global rental/logistics companies.

Customers are consolidating technology portfolios with Unity, leading to improved wallet share and stickiness.

The company just announced a strategic partnership with MTN Group, one of the world's largest telecommunications carriers, which has selected PowerFleet's Unity platform as a white-label solution, providing the foundation for its enterprise data intelligence solutions.

This partnership is significantly increasing the TAM in high-growth, underpenetrated regions, with rollout planned for the second half of the year.

SIXT Rental Mexico is a medium-sized car rental business that will use the Unity platform to optimize fuel/energy efficiency, improve driver accountability, and unlock AI-driven visibility. Another positive is that this deal arrived through referrals and brings higher-than-average ARPUs.

Foley, another new customer win, is a leading US heavy equipment and machinery provider. It is using Unity to solve its fragmented data across mixed-brand fleets, resulting in a 30% reduction in maintenance costs and gains in uptime and efficiency. Unity's OEM-agnostic data ingestion is a key competitive advantage here.

Holcim is one of the world's largest building materials companies, with 9K vehicles across 18 countries, and is yet another big new customer. It achieved an 83% reduction in critical safety events since rolling out Unity across its operations.

The company announced its latest customer win, the Wright Brothers Construction, a medium-sized civil contractor.

Here is some additional data on the increasing momentum of adding new logos:

Channel partnerships are also delivering strong results. Examples are key vehicle leasing partners like SIXT and major global IoT partners like MTN. US channel partners are also delivering most of the AI video bookings.

Several large channel partners like AT&T and TELUS have yet to scale, which are expected to accelerate business inflow in late Q4/early FY27. The company is ahead of schedule with these partnerships.

The company also launched a strategic program with a Fortune 500 demand generation partner to accelerate pipeline growth, already delivering tangible results.

Products

The company's unified operations are a core capability and competitive differentiator (Q1CC, our emphasis):

I want to highlight for our investors some customer value examples of a core capability that's fast becoming a key growth lever for us as well as a strong differentiator from our competitors. We call it unified operations. As you can see from these 3 examples of the large portfolio of third-party system integrations we deliver, we're producing the system of record for customers' operational activities, to power the effectiveness of their overall digital stack, to meaningfully drive performance of their asset, the individual in charge of their assets and the business process... From CIOs, to CFOs, to safety executives, senior stakeholders want our data to pay more effectiveness within their critical applications. They're highly focused on delivering true digital transformation that unified operations can achieve. Each integration you see here is expanding ARPU of between $2 to $8 and creating further intrinsic customer value. This helps us to become mission- critical at the heart of our customers' operations and is a cast iron for durable retention.

PowerFleet was named one of ABI Research's 7 most innovative global technology companies, recognizing its unique platform for enterprise-grade modularity, AI-led innovation (Agentic and generative AI, machine vision), hardware agnostic architecture, rapid deployment, and clear ROI. ABI described Unity as enabling "true digital transformation."

The company launched a new AI risk intervention module inside Unity, which automatically analyzes critical safety events and drives real-time action. This module delivers:

  • Real-time detection of fatigue, distraction, and unsafe behavior.
  • Automated risk alerting and live driver coaching.
  • 80%+ reduction in manual video review hours.
  • Structured export-ready data for insurers and regulators.
  • Measurable ROI to reduce incidents and claims.
  • This AI innovation is already live in the field and driving top-line and bottom-line impact.
  • PowerFleet will host an investor innovation session in November to showcase Unity's AIoT product and technology, demonstrating its agnostic ingestion, harmonization, single pane of glass, and AI-powered applications.

Operations

The company finalized a leaner global operating structure, streamlining decision-making and improving accountability. The phased program to harmonize company-wide systems is advancing, with CRM harmonization across key regions in Q1 basically complete. The integration of MiX is as good as complete.

Q1 expenses included $4.6M in one-time charges for restructuring, integrations, and transaction costs (which are excluded from adjusted EBITDA and EPS).

Risks

There is considerable macro uncertainty producing cautious customers, especially concerning CapEx decisions (which hit mostly the warehouse vertical), and the uncertainty could very well increase going forward.

So it's not surprising that sales cycles have lengthened for segments requiring considerable CapEx-laden product deals.

Management is also facing a rapidly changing tariff landscape and has acted fast by limiting exposures by rebalancing supplier exposure, negotiating improved cost rates, and managing impacts. However, this could not prevent tariffs from adversely impacting product margins.

Product revenue was down, partly due to tariff uncertainty and a $2M sequential decline due to a change in accounting.

Financials

The acquisition effect is lapping out of the quarterly growth figures:

  • Revenue was up 38% $104M for the quarter, a 38% increase year-over-year, and approximately $1M higher than consensus estimates.

  • Service revenue was up 53% y/y and 6% sequentially to $86.5M, reaching a record 83% of total revenue, up from 79% in Q1 '25, marking a continued shift towards SaaS.

  • Adjusted EBITDA was up 58% to $21.6M, exceeding consensus by over $1M. This strong performance is attributed to synergy realization, platform consolidation, and a SaaS-centric revenue mix.
  • Service-adjusted EBITDA gross margin hit 76%, validating the improved efficiency of the model.
  • Adjusted EBITDA gross margins reached 67%, a 300bp expansion driven by a stronger revenue mix and services strength.
  • Product margins were 25%, impacted by tariffs, which tempered CapEx deals.
  • The company produced $11M of annualized savings in Q1 out of the committed $18M for the full year FY26, $7M of which came from implementing a leaner global operating structure as of April 1. Additional savings resulted from disciplined cost control (stuff like execution in vendor spend reduction, eliminating redundant applications, aligning to a global support ticketing system, and facilities consolidation). Management is confident in delivering the remaining $7M of annualized EBITDA savings.
  • These savings are reflected in a rapidly improving operating cash flow, although free cash flow is still negative (mostly due to CapEx, including capitalized software development).

  • Net debt was $235M at quarter-end, up from $229M at fiscal 2025-end, which was in line with expectations of front-loaded investments for platform integration and synergy capture. The net debt-to-EBITDA ratio exited Q1 at 2.97x, down from 3.2x at the end of FY25.
  • Management guides for an under 2.25x net leverage by year-end, projecting an additional $30M in net debt improvement in H2, driven by top-line growth, reduced CapEx intensity, and improved working capital.

Outlook

  • Product margins are expected to remain in the mid-20% range.
  • Sales and marketing expenses are expected to run at approximately 18% of revenue to support capability expansion.
  • The company expects 10% organic services growth as it exits the year, reaching double-digit.
  • Longer-term ambition is for 85% plus SaaS revenue as part of the overall mix, with true SaaS margins of 90% to 95%.
  • Growth in services was primarily ARPU-driven, with only a modest change upwards in subscribers. While the average services ARPU is around $15, the company is focused on ARPU expansion, with in-warehouse and AI video solutions yielding ARPU from $30 up to $125.

We've bolded the latter, as that seems to suggest significant upside, as AI video solutions were growing strongly, although tempering this somewhat is that the demand comes from the warehouse vertical, which is the most CapEx intensive and hence the most impacted by tariffs and macro uncertainty.

Valuation

There are 1.66M outstanding options (there are 5.17M more, but these have a $13.85 exercise price) and 130K warrants for a fully diluted share count of 135.2M which at $5.5 per share delivers a market cap of $608.4M and an EV of $843.4M.

With an expected FY26 (ending in March 2026) revenue of $432.5M this produces a 1.95x EV/S. The company is still expected to produce a small (EPS of negative $0.03) in FY26, swinging to a 27-cent per share profit in FY27.

Conclusion

  • The scaled-up platform is now playing in the A-League, attracting big customers and partners.
  • The shift towards SaaS is ongoing, now reaching 83% of revenue, boosting gross margins.
  • The company continues to introduce new products.
  • There is significant upside to ARPU (Average Revenue Per Unit), especially from AI video solutions.
  • The cost synergies are emerging, so with continued revenue growth due to improved scale and competitive position, this should lead to significantly improved financials.
  • We see an inflection point in the company, but not in the share price.

data.title: Prologis: The Logistics Leader With High Data Center Ambitions To Watch
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Prologis (PLD) is a REIT that stands as the global leader in logistics real estate, specializing in the ownership, development, and management of high-quality logistics facilities, playing an important role in the economy. In recent years, the company has significantly ramped up their growth with an aggressive acquisition and development strategy, which might have good potential to compound thanks to a new cycle of market rent growth and data center interest, so let’s see if there’s an opportunity at this price.

We can see an overall nice spread when it comes to the type of goods/services sold in their buildings, with transport/freight being the largest (~12%), and an overall solid top 10 of customers such as Amazon (AMZN), Home Depot (HD), FedEx (FDX), Deutsche Post (OTCPK:DHLGY), etc., as we can expect from this type of company.

They've made ~$4.71 billion in AFFO at their peak, but due to an overall weaker environment and mounting interest rates - as I mention throughout the article - the AFFO might be overall relatively flat compared to 2024. Still, the company has recently raised their core FFO guidance midpoint after beating Q2 expectations thanks to a record high leasing pipeline and an increasingly ready-to-act customer base, according to their CEO, expecting an occupancy rate of around 94.25% to 94.75%. As they say, the record high pipeline of leases combined with a 20% spread between market and replacement cost rents could come with the next cycle of market rent growth as the demand improves.

We see a massive increase in debt (more than tripled since 2018) as the company is investing significant amounts of money into both acquisitions and property development while also expanding into data centers and renewable energy. They purchased Liberty Properties Trust for $12.6 billion (all-stock) and Industrial Property Trust for $4 billion (cash) back in 2020, Duke Realty back in 2022 in an all-stock deal worth $26 billion, followed by a Blackstone (BX) portfolio worth $3.1 billion (using cash) in 2023, among others, which justifies the increase as the company is expanding significantly.

Financially, based on their latest report, we see an overall acceptable position for a REIT that’s been developing so much. Although the debt has been going up during this time, so did the company’s assets, and this is an important aspect to remember about REITs. Since they have to pay a majority of what they make as dividends to retain their REIT tax status, that means the company has to outsource the capital, be it from issuing debt, issuing shares - both of which they’ve done significantly, as mentioned above - or selling assets. Still, this is a manageable amount of debt overall and it leaves us with $57.3 billion in equity, which isn’t great for a $107.5 billion market cap, but it’s important to remember that the balance sheet is backward-looking and doesn’t take into account the value of their properties in today’s market, which might be significantly higher, as I’ll mention below.

Dividends & Buybacks

Although they’ve been growing the dividend to account for the higher number of shares and investments paying out, a ~3.55% yield that costs them ~$3.81 billion per year is not that good, especially since it represents a payout of ~86.2% based on last year’s AFFO. As a REIT, not even beating treasuries, especially in this environment where some peers are even at double PLD’s yield, is simply bad, although there’s still potential to see this yield go down as the stock would go up thanks to rate cuts, as I will mention below. Still, we’re very likely looking at a premium being assumed for the industry leader given their aggressive development strategy and international expansion potential.

Risks & Opportunities

The rise of e-commerce seen during the pandemic was likely only a boost into what will continue to be a significant tailwind for PLD. This will continue to add to the value of their real estate and allow them to ask for better rent on their leases over the long-term, but we also have to think about e-commerce in the current environment, which I’ll detail in a bit.

As they say, they’re diversified enough to benefit from a diverse set of demand drivers, with ~38% of their customers benefitting from the steady and stable basic daily needs, ~30% from cyclical spending patterns (pandemic boosted this as well) and ~32% from structural trends such as e-commerce directly, all of which are improved by a growing population with more digital access and different spending habits than older generations.

They also offer an interesting bit of information regarding e-commerce, saying that it requires about 4x more square feet of space for the same amount of sales. This comes as a result of the relatively limited product portfolio of brick-and-mortar stores, which comes with different inventory/storage requirements, but also the need for much greater inventory than traditional stores, much bigger volumes/warehouse activity, and reverse logistics.

With these trends, it’s no surprise that PLD will rely on the economy quite a lot, which is both a risk and an opportunity - depending mostly on when we buy and what our goals are. As I keep mentioning as the main near-term catalyst as a REIT - especially one in the industrial sector - probably their highest potential near-term catalyst will be rate cuts. As I mentioned, we have to think about e-commerce and the business as a whole in the current environment. The role of rate cuts is to slow down the economy to fight rising inflation (which came after the significant money printing seen during the pandemic), reducing economic activity by making debt more expensive (for both people and companies) and lowering discretionary budgets.

However, rate cuts will be very beneficial. First, they’d help the company directly by reducing the cost of debt if they refinance or directly lower their variable interest payments - with interest payments currently at a massive $251.86 million in Q2 alone, and a record $946 million over the past 12 months - but it would also help their tenants significantly, boosting industrial activity. This way, it will be easier for companies to invest, while rate cuts would also boost the economy, improve spending and overall economic activity, since that is the role of this tool from the Fed’s perspective. High rates slow the economy to fight inflation, low rates boost it - and they are both natural parts of the economic cycle. Especially with rate cuts being more and more likely, especially after Powell’s Jackson Hole remarks, we’re very likely to see this catalyst materialize soon, which will have a clear effect on the company and its stock - and many others.

Not only that, but rate cuts will also be beneficial for dividend stocks in particular, as we can see in the market’s reaction to the previous cuts, since money would be required to risk up if it wants good yields. We’ve had US treasuries (which are considered the risk-free assets) trading at 4-5% yields for so long, which should theoretically have a negative effect on the demand for dividend stocks, as money would follow the better risk-adjusted yields. I don’t believe we’ll get back to a zero-rate environment any time soon, but a reduction in rates is nonetheless beneficial.

Also, tariffs could be bad at first if they hurt the economy, but I believe one of their goals is bringing some production back to the US, which would be great news for Prologis and its peers, and the company stands to gain a lot from it thanks to their size. Still, the market adapting to this would take time, and until then we’re likely to see negative effects such as a slowdown in activity that PLD has reported back in May due to trade policy uncertainty, and it could also have rippling effects in the economy due to companies raising prices (inflation) to offset some of the effects of tariffs, be they direct or indirect, by increasing commodity/material prices.

Right now, we’re looking at significant growth in rent since the pandemic boost. As reported by other peers that I analyzed in previous articles - such as Rexford Industrial Realty (REXR) or STAG Industrial (STAG) - the past couple of years saw a decline, especially for industrial warehouse values, leading to somewhat of a normalization that may soon be about to revert into more growth. Regarding REXR, we recently saw Elliott Investment Management reportedly taking a stake in them, which could be the signal for a lot of interest from the market in industrial REITs, although it’s unclear yet what their intentions are.

Also, as PLD is the leader in its sector, they grew to a point where the US wasn’t enough, expanding internationally over the years. This comes with a lot of potential, as getting exposure to LATAM or SEA, for example, can offer a lot of growth in the future, as these regions develop. Plus, domestic rent continues to dominate their NOI (85%), so there’s significant room to expand, which is something we’ve seen from Realty Income (O) as well over the past decade - which so far has expanded mainly in Europe.

And one final aspect to keep in mind as a potential safety net is that in case we get into some fundamental changes that would decrease the need for their logistic centers, these properties have the potential to be repurposed. For example, warehouses can be turned into data centers quite easily, which is actually quite common in the industry and offers “lower acquisition and construction costs than greenfield sites, as well as aid shorter time to market, whilst offering location benefits,” according to some insights from Cushman & Wakefield. Given the huge amount of demand for data centers we might see over the following decade, as a majority of Big Tech globally is now planning to invest hundreds of billions in 2025 alone for AI, data centers, and cloud, we might see significant potential for PLD’s properties going forward. In fact, Prologis is actively doing this already (they have a long-term vision of converting over 100 warehouses into data centers) and has a team dedicated to identifying suitable conversion opportunities, so being aware of this and taking action is definitely a plus for them.

Peer Comparison

Compared to peers such as Lineage (LINE), Rexford Industrial Realty, EastGroup Properties (EGP), First Industrial Realty Trust (FR) and STAG Industrial, we see overall higher ratios for PLD in almost everything, but nothing too outlandish to signal some potential overvaluation. It’s also worth noting that PLD is about 10 times bigger than even the largest of these peers, so their size as the market leader should also be kept in mind, as it comes with much better power of negotiation/credit.

Valuation

Starting with last year’s $4.42 billion AFFO, I’ll expect a 5.5% CAGR over the first 5 years (below the 7.1% expected by the market to be more conservative), followed by 4% for the subsequent 5 years and a 3% terminal growth rate, as their international expansion still has significant potential for decades, as well as a WACC of 7%, in line with my own estimates based on a ~7.6% cost of equity, ~5% cost of debt, and 0% tax rate since they're a REIT. This way, the enterprise value gets to ~$144.17 billion, and after subtracting the $33.6 billion in net debt, this returns an equity value of ~$110.57 billion. With 955.88 million shares outstanding, this gives an intrinsic value of about $115.67 per share, which is almost identical to the current share price.

Conclusions

Overall, Prologis seems to be fairly valued for quite a bit of growth ahead. Since I believe there are better opportunities in the sector right now that should benefit from the exact same trends but also have much more potential to expand plus even double the dividends - such as One Liberty Properties (OLP) covered recently - I rate PLD a Hold now, as their valuation is fair, although the potential does exist, so it's worth adding to the watchlist.

data.title: The Bank of New York Mellon: Transformation Into A High-Margin Financial Platform Signals Strong Stock Upside
data.description:
The Bank of New York Mellon (BNY Mellon) (BK) stock investment case focused on its ongoing transformation from a traditional custodian into a high-margin and capital-light financial platform. BNY Mellon’s Q2 2025 revenue growth and ~5% points of operating leverage gain reflect that the strategic shift derived large value (for the stock) and led to a ~28% ROTCE. Thus, the rating for the stock is a buy.

The reason behind this rating is that the market has started to price in (not fully yet) the possibilities (like fee-based revenue growth and rapid bottom-line uplift) of this business transformation. BNY Mellon has constantly delivered on its high-return targets with efficiency gains through its new operating model. With that, its valuation multiple can expand, and ongoing re-rating towards a premium is more in line with BNY Mellon as a market infrastructure provider (than a bank). There is a possibility for forward multiple expansion amid a stable 100% capital return policy that backs tangible book value growth.

Major risks to this thesis include execution risk in turning around the underperforming Investment and Wealth Management segment, a macro downturn that can directly impact its market-dependent fee revenues, and increasing regulatory scrutiny (on its M&A moves) that can limit BNY Mellon’s strategic options/targets.

BNY Mellon Stock’s Continued Outperformance with Strong Uptrend and Bullish Price Targets

The BNY Mellon stock delivered a solid outperformance against benchmarks. Over the last five-year period, BNY Mellon stock delivered a total return of 224.9%. The stock’s performance outpaced both its direct benchmark [the Invesco KBW Bank ETF (KBWB) that returned 122.3%] and the market benchmark [the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) returned 93.74%]. During 2021–Mid-2023, BNY Mellon's stock moved in close correlation with the banking sector ETF (similar peaks and drops).

Since early 2024, BNY Mellon's stock has been in an upward trend. BK stock pricing has shifted away from both the banking sector and the S&P 500. This divergence in total return performance happened along with specific catalysts and business shifts (business transformation augment). BK stock’s outperformance continued in 2025, with the 1Y total return marking a high of 226.4%. There is a solid momentum (as of now) that is leaving benchmarks far behind BK stock.

In technical terms, BNY Mellon stock is also in a strong uptrend that started in mid-2023. BK stock is currently trading just inside the resistance zone after reaching a swing high of ~$107. BK stock price is getting close to a partial/swing profit-booking level amid an extended healthy price rally. BNY Mellon's stock price remains well above the modified exponential moving averages (trend lines) that will continue to act as dynamic support throughout the trend ascent.

In the continued bullish scenario (till Q2-2026), Fibonacci extensions are used to project potential forward price targets (if the uptrend continues). These "Extensions" serve as logical areas where the stock might face resistance or where traders might take profits. The main upside levels identified are $125.5 [near the midpoint of $121.05 (0.236 extension) and $130.80 (0.382 extension)]. A major resistance is projected at $157-$157.85 (near the 0.786 extension) that can be considered as a primary target for "partial profit-booking."

In the corrective scenario, should the stock pull back from its current price levels, the Fibonacci retracement levels may serve as possible support zones with buying interest, where large "accumulation," may emerge. The main downside levels are primary support around $90.15-$89.45 (near 0.236 retracement) that would be a minor and healthy price pullback. A more important support level is at $79.5-$78 (just above the 0.50 retracement, the lower end of the current price channel) if a deeper correction happens but can still be considered a buying opportunity within the long-term uptrend context.

In terms of earnings-based valuation (forward P/E multiple analysis), the consensus EPS estimates are used. These are $7.87 for FY2026 and $8.83 for FY2027. Whereas the current forward non-GAAP P/E (~14.9x), 5-year average P/E (~11.2x), and sector median P/E (~11.5x) can be used as P/E multiple justifications. The current multiple already reflects a premium to its historical average and the sector. This indicates market optimism about BNY Mellon’s business transformation. The main valuation question is whether this premium is stable and has more room to expand.

For the base case, a 15x PE is justified, as it is reflecting the current market sentiment for BNY Mellon stock. The market may continue to appreciate constant execution with a stable premium multiple. Based on the PE formula, $118 and $132 are the price targets for 2026 and 2027. Similarly, for the bull case, an 18.6x (based on a potential match of price/earnings to growth ratio of 1 of BNY in line with the sector median of 1.24, a 24% expansion) can be assumed if BNY successfully re-rates as a high-margin platform, justifying a valuation closer to leading fintech/market infrastructure guys that leads to $146 and $164 pricing for 2026 and 2027.

Finally, for the Bear Case, an 11.2x is assumed to reflect that if growth stalls and operating leverage turns negative, the stock's multiple compresses back toward its historical average. This stance provides BNY Mellon stock prices at $88 and $99 for 2026 and 2027.

Robust Growth in 2025 with Upgraded NII Guidance and Strong Fee Revenue Momentum

A primary driver of the bullish argument on BNY Mellon stock is the company’s upgraded financial outlook and the clear momentum built in H1-2025. Management has raised its FY2025 guidance for net interest income (NII) growth from an original estimate of "mid-single-digits" to "high-single-digits" Y/Y. This 2025 NII revision is supported by the 17% Y/Y increase in NII to $1.203 billion in Q2. The increased NII in Q2 was a result of the continued reinvestment of maturing securities at higher yields and balance sheet growth. Average deposits grew 6% Q/Q to $300.3 billion. The deposit growth fueled a 6% Q/Q increase in average interest-earning assets to $375.5 billion. With that, BNY Mellon can attract and deploy capital effectively in a favorable rate environment that may continue to yield strong, predictable earnings.

Beyond NII, the company projects "solid fee revenue growth," and fees constitute ~72% of total revenue. This forecast is solidified by two consecutive quarters of record sales that are a direct outcome of the new commercial model activated a year prior. In Q2, total fee revenue grew 7% Y/Y to $3.641 billion, with investment services fees surging 9%. This fee surge indicates a scaled ability to capture market beta and drive organic alpha. With that, BNY Mellon is reducing business dependency on market conditions alone. The most potent metric under this momentum is the attainment of ~5% points of positive operating leverage in Q2. This operating leverage came from total revenue growth of 9% along with lower expense growth of 4%. Such a big gap between revenue and expense growth is a signal of increasing forward profitability and efficiency, directly leading to the improved pretax margin of 37% and a ROTCE of 27.8%.

Transformation and Digital Asset Advancements to Drive Significant Growth

BNY Mellon’s strategic transformation that is focused on a "platform's operating model" is a long-term catalyst for the stock price upside. The shift is way beyond a simple cost-cutting exercise. It is a complete realignment of how BNY Mellon works to scale commercial opportunities and accelerate speed to market. With over half of BNY Mellon's employees now transitioned into this model. Early results from the initial "Wave 1" groups are leading to faster delivery times, improved service quality, and increased product advancements. The full benefits of this model materialize after business units have been operating within it for a full year. This transformation creates a clear forward timeline for value uplift, as the remaining 50% of transitions may be done over the next year. So if the entire BNY Mellon matures within this new framework, the efficiency gains and revenue synergies may accelerate decisively in 2026 and in following years.

Moreover, the model fundamentally uplifts BNY Mellon's M&A capability. This "clarity of chassis" (transcript) transforms M&A for BNY Mellon from a potentially risky effort into a more reliable tool for accelerating growth, like the one observed in the successful integration of Archer. Advancements in digital assets and AI base also solidify the bullish outlook. For instance, BNY Mellon's recent selection by Societe Generale and Ripple to serve as the reserve custodian for their USD stablecoins is a validation of its digital assets platform advancement. This moves BNY Mellon beyond traditional custody into a leader in servicing the growing tokenized economy. The progress is also supported by a deal with Goldman Sachs to use blockchain for the record-keeping of money market funds. This deal may unlock money market funds’ utility as a form of collateral and open up easier transferability. As a result, this will create potential revenue for BNY Mellon.

Valuation Re-Rating Can Be Driven by AI Adoption and Strong Capital Management

Internally, the enterprise-wide adoption of the Eliza AI platform and digital employees signals a deep focus on future productivity. The immediate profit and loss impact is modest, but the management has explicitly guided for an "acceleration in '26, '27, '28, '29 and beyond." (See transcript.) This strategy is using AI as more than a cost-saving tool, as a means to increase capacity to focus on higher-value, client-facing activities. The focus can drive forward organic growth that is not reflected in the analysts’ revenue and EPS estimates (in terms of YoY growth). But in the last three months, consensus estimates for the next fiscal year-end have seen 11 upward revisions for EPS and 10 for revenue, with zero downward revisions. This positive sentiment from the analysts points out that they do not see any adverse performance points at BNY Mellon, and combined with long-term consensus EPS growth projections extending to 2029, it provides a strong quantitative underpinning for future stock price appreciation.

I am not blindly following what the management is saying. But if we analyze BNY Mellon’s capital management, returns provide a clear basis for forward confidence. I mean, the Board of Directors' approval of a 13% increase in the quarterly dividend (to common stock) to $0.53 per share (effective August 2025) is a direct signal of stability in its earnings power. Moreover, this dividend is along with the plan to maintain a total payout ratio of ~100% for 2025 (transcript) that includes an active share repurchase program that returned $895 million to stockholders in Q2 alone. The 27.8% ROTCE attained in the quarter should not be seen as a peak. To answer why? The CEO stated that they "don't see a ceiling on that number." With three-quarters being capital-light, BNY Mellon's profitability profile may increasingly align with that of a high-return-platforms company. Overall, these points can drive a large re-rating of the stock's valuation multiple over the long term (this is what I observe here).

Execution Risks and Regulatory Hurdles Amid Strategic Transformation

The chimes for BNY Mellon stock upside have a primary issue: execution risk within the Investment and Wealth Management segment. BNY Mellon holds record results, but this division had a 2% Y/Y revenue decline to $801 million and experienced gigantic net outflows of $17 billion in Q2. This underperformance was attributed to the mix of AUM flows and an adjustment for certain rebates and occurred against a backdrop of rising markets. So the market can raise questions about its position and product appeal. Although a new leader is in place with a strategy to better link the firm’s strong "manufacturing" capabilities with its vast distribution channels like Pershing, turnarounds in asset management are not guaranteed. Continued outflows and margin pressure in this segment (a pretax margin of 19% against the firm-wide 37%) can act as a big drag on consolidated results and the transformation-related sentiment. This issue is also reflected in BNY Mellon's guidance. The full-year fee revenue forecast of simply "up" feels a bit conservative given the 5% growth already attained in H1. Even though management cites market dependency for this, it can also signal internal uncertainty about stable momentum across all fee-generating businesses (mainly in investment management).

Apart from that, ongoing regulatory and geopolitical headwinds can bring in material risk to BNY Mellon's strategic M&A moves. The letter from Senator Elizabeth Warren holds a warning that a potential merger with Northern Trust may raise "serious antitrust concerns." This development points forward to the political and regulatory scrutiny tilt on any large-scale M&A in the custody banking sector. Even CEO Robin Vince stated the bar for large transactions is "super high," this external pressure effectively limits a major potential avenue (M&A) for transformative inorganic growth. With that, these headwinds may continue to force BNY Mellon to have a greater dependence on a harder-to-drive organic growth.

Furthermore, the CEO's own acknowledgment of "elevated risks from geopolitical tensions and conflicts as well as uncertainty around trade, fiscal, and other policies" (transcript) is a caveat that is hard to ignore. As a central node in the financial system, BNY Mellon's ops are directly sensitive to market volatility and shifts in client activity due to macro factors. A major macro downturn and related period of risk aversion can hurt transaction volumes and assets under custody/administration (a massive $55.8 trillion), directly pressuring the 75% of revenue ("market dependent" fee-based revenue).

From a market perception standpoint, the reported sale of ~$2 million worth of stock by the Senior EVP and General Counsel, Kevin McCarthy, can be viewed negatively (as they are not automatic as per Nasdaq data). However, this is not a sudden signal transaction, he is selling stock at this size (considering historical data, I am not surprised). Insider sales can have many personal motivations, but a significant sale by a top executive during a period of strong stock performance can be interpreted (not by all but by a fraction of the investor base) as a signal that insiders see limited near-term upside.

To conclude, BNY Mellon stock is a buy (no doubt), as it is transitioning from a misunderstood bank into a high-return financial platform. The transformation is real as per the shifts in financials, like ~28% ROTCE and positive operating leverage. The market has begun to reward this shift (decisively in 2025), but the valuation re-rating toward a premium market infrastructure multiple is not complete yet and is a clear long opportunity. For the investment stance, I always support the phased accumulation strategy. To be straight, initiate a starter long position at current price levels to capture strong momentum and reserve capital to build the core holding on any large pullbacks toward the primary technical support zones.

Here, forward monitorables include operating leverage [continued quarterly mark of revenue growth outpacing expense growth], performance of the Investment & Wealth Management Segment [stabilization of net flows and signs of margin improvement], and capital returns [stability of ~100% total payout policy and tangible book value growth]. In short, BK stock is a core long-term Growth at a Reasonable Price (GARP) case based on a structural shift in the business model that yields growth with possible valuation multiple expansion.

data.title: Big European Banks Up Q2 Provisions As Economic, Geopolitical Risks Persist
data.description:
European lenders set aside more capital for loan losses in the second quarter as the asset quality pressure from an economic slowdown and geopolitical issues persisted.

The aggregate quarterly loan loss provisions of the region's largest banks amounted to €10.94 billion, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data. This represents a 0.7% increase quarter-over-quarter and about 12.2% year-over-year.

Rising geopolitical risks and the impact of higher US tariffs on the global economy should continue to weigh on European banks' balance sheets and may result in a mild deterioration in their asset quality, Scope Ratings wrote in an Aug. 14 note.

Market Intelligence's sample included European banks with assets of more than €100 billion at the end of June, except those in Russia and Ukraine.

Türkiye Cumhuriyeti Ziraat Bankasi AS's problem loan ratio increased by 18 basis points quarter-over-quarter, the highest in the sample. Ziraat Bank is the largest lender by assets in Turkey, which has seen some domestic political tensions since March. The country's central bank has resumed monetary easing with a surprise 300-basis point cut in July to spur economic growth.

Germany's top two listed lenders, Deutsche Bank AG (DB) and Commerzbank AG (OTCPK:CRZBF)(OTCPK:CRZBY), booked increases of 16 bps and 10 bps, respectively. German Chancellor Friedrich Merz has said the economy faces a "structural crisis" due to high energy costs and the effects of high US tariffs. The country's banks also carry significant exposures to the real estate sector, particularly Deutsche Bank, which is substantially exposed to US commercial real estate.

Deutsche Bank CFO James von Moltke said the bank's CRE provisions in the quarter remained elevated, noting that there were valuation pressures on nonperforming exposures, particularly in the US West Coast.

Commerzbank set aside an additional €270 million in the quarter to cover potential losses tied to economic factors, including US tariffs, climate change and sector-specific risks.

Italy-based UniCredit SpA (OTCPK:UNCFF)(OTCPK:UNCRY) and Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena SpA (OTCPK:BMDPF) posted some of the largest quarterly declines in problem loan ratios among the banks in the sample. However, Monte dei Paschi remained the bank with the highest ratio at 3.74% at the end of June.

US tariffs continue to pose a risk to the asset quality of the Italian banking sector due to the country's reliance on exports, Morningstar DBRS said in a commentary. Yet, banks' income diversification strategies, higher liquidity and improved capital buffers should help absorb credit losses from these risks.

For stage 2 loans, or underperforming loans at increased risk of turning sour, HSBC Holdings PLC (HSBC) recorded the sharpest quarterly increase in the sample, followed by Belgium's KBC Group NV (OTCPK:KBCSF)(OTCPK:KBCSY) and Austria's Raiffeisen Bank International AG (OTCPK:RAIFF)(OTCPK:RAIFY).

HSBC said in its interim report that the increase in stage 2 loans was mainly caused by updates to its models in calculating the probability of default. As a result, some performing loans got shifted to the stage 2 bucket, although they are mainly of "good" and "satisfactory quality." The bank expects its 2025 expected credit losses, as a percentage of average gross loans, to hit about 40 bps and settle at the upper end of its medium-term target range of 30-40 bps.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Opendoor: Stagnant Inventory And Weak Guidance Signal A Clear Sell (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Opendoor Technologies (OPEN) is up 240% since my last evaluation in July titled, “Why Opendoor’s Huge Surge Needs a Reality Check (And Why It Might Last).”

Although OPEN’s surge is predominantly a function of meme-stock dynamics, there had been signs of a turnaround (e.g., improved operational efficiency and reduced losses). Moreover, Opendoor’s pivot from an asset-heavy iBuying model to an asset-light, agent-assisted model is a cause for optimism, although considerable risks remain (e.g., persistent losses, a challenging real estate market).

Recent Developments

Immediately following its Q2 report in early August, OPEN's stock price declined by ~20%.

Metric/Guidance Q2 2025 Result Analyst Estimate Surprise My Commentary

Q3 Adjusted EBITDA Guidance –$28M to –$21M N/A N/A Guides to renewed losses
Profitability Outlook N/A N/A N/A No profitability expected in 2H 2025
Investor Takeaway — — — Q2 "profit" was a one-off; business model challenges persist

Just days later, Opendoor’s CEO, Carrie Wheeler, suddenly stepped down. There did not appear to be a planned, extended succession, which should sound alarms in the market regarding the company’s confidence, or lack thereof, in its turnaround plan, although the news was framed as “in support of [the] next phase of growth and innovation.”

It’s important to keep in mind that Opendoor’s top-line figures (especially revenue) are lagging indicators of business health. So, we turn to its key performance indicators, which reveal that Opendoor’s iBuying engine, as expected, is sputtering.
